item.title: Democrat New Jersey governor says thousands of noncitizens discovered on voter rolls, purged
item.description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill says a software error in New Jersey's motor vehicle system registered roughly 6,600 noncitizens to vote, and some cast ballots.
item.content:encoded:
New Jersey's governor announced Tuesday afternoon that thousands of noncitizens were registered to vote in her state — and some voted in past elections.

Democratic Gov. Mikie Sherrill said that a "serious software error in New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle System led to the registration of roughly 6,600 people who indicated that they were not U.S. citizens between June 2023 and June 2024, almost three years prior to my taking office."

"These individuals answered 'no' when asked on a keypad whether they were a U.S. citizen when applying for drivers' licenses and identification cards, but through no fault of their own, the system registered them anyway," according to Sherrill.

CALIFORNIA ACCUSED OF BLOCKING FEDERAL VOTER ROLL AUDIT AS DOJ ESCALATES PROBE OF ELECTION FRAUD CLAIMS

She stressed that while the "error" occurred under the previous administration, headed by Democrat Phil Murphy, she ordered her administration's attorney to look into the matter and remove the illegally-registered voters from the rolls.

"Our preliminary analysis shows fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted," she said. "They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state."

"I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time," she said, emphasizing for the third time that the improper voting didn't happen on her watch.

NJ TAXPAYERS ON THE HOOK FOR $12M MORE AS DEM GOVERNOR PROTECTS ILLEGAL ALIENS BATTLING DEPORTATION

In the same message admitting that people in her state improperly voted, Sherrill then attacked President Donald Trump for what she described as spreading lies about legitimacy of American elections.

An elected New Jersey official slammed Democrats for failing on the issue of election integrity.

"This is another example of Democrats failing to secure elections. If Democrats would embrace voter ID – which has broad public support – the MVC’s voter registration failures would have been corrected at the ballot box," said NJ Assembly Minority Leader John DiMaio, R-Hackettstown. "New Jersey needs voter ID, because citizens deserve confidence in election results and Trenton Democrats have proven they do not respond to known problems in the voter rolls."

The Republican National Committee's spokesperson on election integrity similarly slammed the New Jersey governor for her classification of the issue.

"Mikie Sherrill says election integrity is 'fundamental to our democracy,' yet just last month the RNC and NJGOP uncovered hundreds of noncitizens on New Jersey's voter rolls and records of noncitizens voting in past elections," Ally Trilio told Fox News Digital.

"Every time Democrats say noncitizen voting is a 'non-issue,' voters learn about another case that proves otherwise. It's time to stop making excuses and pass the SAVE America Act."

In June, Fox News Digital exclusively reported that noncitizens were registered to vote in the state over a period of years. Some of them voted in elections. Others, when attempting to become naturalized citizens, copped to being on the voter rolls and said they were registered by mistake.

The New Jersey Republican Party (NJGOP) and the Republican National Committee (RNC) requested voter rolls from all 21 counties in the Garden State and found multiple instances of noncitizens seeking naturalization asking to be removed from the rolls, claiming they were unknowingly registered to vote. Most were registered as Democrats.

RNC LEGAL VICTORY CLEARS NORTH CAROLINA VOTER ROLL PURGE OF NONCITIZENS THROUGH JURY RESPONSES

In official letters viewed by Fox News Digital from Atlantic County, Superintendent of Elections & Commissioner of Registration Maureen Bugdon certified that noncitizens came to her asking to be removed.

"Please allow this letter to confirm that on today's date, the below referenced individual came before this office to confirm her registration and voter status," a typical letter read. "She relayed that she did not wish to be a New Jersey registered voter and does not understand how she became registered through the Department of Motor Vehicles, allegedly."

Other documents showed noncitizens directly asking to be removed from the rolls through a state voter registration cancellation form.

When prompted about why they wished to be removed, the vast majority of the unknowingly registered voters checked a box labeled "other" and wrote that they were not citizens.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Federal Election Commission.

item.title: Texas real estate agent suspected in nearly 100-stab cold case posted global vacations before arrest
item.description: Surveillance video from 2012 showed a hooded figure entering Irasema Chavez's apartment the night before she was found stabbed roughly 100 times.
item.content:encoded:
More than a decade after a Texas woman was found stabbed roughly 100 times in her apartment, a real estate agent is behind bars without bond, facing capital murder charges.

Mayra Velasquez, 42, was booked into the Tarrant County Corrections Center on July 17 in connection with the killing of Irasema Chavez, according to the Arlington Police Department and county jail records.

Chavez, 32, was found dead Jan. 20, 2012, inside her second-floor apartment in the 2700 block of South Collins Street. Investigators said there were no obvious signs of forced entry, raising the possibility that Chavez knew the person who killed her or willingly opened the door.

DNA CRACKS 40-YEAR COLD CASE MYSTERY WITH ARREST IN LIBRARY WORKER’S SAVAGE SLAYING: POLICE

Surveillance video obtained by KDFW-TV, from the night before the grisly discovery showed a person wearing baggy clothing and a hoodie climbing the stairs to Chavez’s unit. The figure knocked, appeared to exchange words with someone at the door and was then allowed inside.

Police released the video in hopes the public could identify the visitor, but the shadowy image failed to produce the break detectives needed. The case eventually went cold.

FLORIDA WOMAN CHARGED WITH MURDER EVADING POLICE AFTER BODY FOUND IN SHALLOW GRAVE, SHERIFF SAYS

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

Investigators did, however, recover blood from the crime scene that did not belong to Chavez.

In 2016, Arlington police worked with Virginia-based Parabon NanoLabs to analyze the unknown DNA. The company used DNA phenotyping technology to produce a composite predicting the person’s likely appearance, including long brown hair and Latino ancestry.

FORENSIC GENEALOGY UNMASKS COLD CASE SUSPECT AS STRANGLER, SEXUAL PREDATOR DECADES LATER: OFFICIALS

Still, no firm suspect emerged.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

The case gained new life in November 2024, when Arlington police brought it to the FBI’s Dallas Field Office and sought help from the bureau’s investigative genetic genealogy program. That process produced what authorities described as a significant lead earlier this year.

Detectives then gathered additional evidence and obtained a warrant for Velasquez, police said.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Authorities have not released what evidence confirmed the genealogy lead, whether investigators obtained a direct DNA comparison from Velasquez or if she knew Chavez.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

Velasquez had worked locally as a real estate agent for years, posting property listings on her social media accounts.

Her Texas real estate license remained active as of Monday, and state records listed her with Pure Realtors LLC, KDFW-TV reported. Fox News Digital has reached out to the company for comment.

SEND US A TIP

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Velasquez's social media account captured the real estate agent gallivanting on trips with family and friends following the alleged murder.

She was seen smiling in trips to Peru, Mexico, Paris, and London.

GOT A TIP?

Tarrant County’s inmate database lists Velasquez’s charge as "capital murder by terror threat/other felony."

Arlington police and the FBI are expected to address the arrest during a joint news conference Wednesday at 10:30 a.m. CST. Fox News Digital has reached out to Velasquez’s attorney for comment.

item.title: FBI nabs alleged $547M Medicare fraud fugitive overseas, third capture from 'Most Wanted Fraudsters' list
item.description: Khalid Ahmed Satary allegedly billed Medicare $547 million for unnecessary genetic tests on elderly patients before fleeing the country in 2022.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: The FBI has captured a fugitive accused of stealing more than half a billion dollars from Medicare and leading authorities on an international manhunt, marking the third arrest from the bureau's "Most Wanted Fraudsters" list.

Khalid Ahmed Satary, who was indicted in 2019 in the Southern District of Florida, was arrested overseas after spending nearly three years on the run, according to the FBI. Authorities said Satary violated the conditions of his pretrial release in 2022, prompting a federal arrest warrant.

"The arrest of Khalid Ahmed Satary and return to the U.S. is the third Most Wanted Fraudster capture from this FBI and our partners in just five weeks — continuing the historic run of success for this new initiative," FBI Director Kash Patel told Fox News Digital in a statement.

FBI CAPTURES $1.2B MEDICARE FRAUD FUGITIVE IN PHILIPPINES, SECOND ARREST FROM MOST WANTED FRAUDSTERS LIST

Patel said Satary is accused of participating in a conspiracy that fraudulently billed Medicare for $547 million worth of expensive and medically unnecessary genetic tests between 2016 and 2019. According to the FBI, the scheme relied on deceptive marketing campaigns, illegal kickbacks and bribes to generate fraudulent Medicare claims.

"This defendant allegedly orchestrated a massive fraud scheme that preyed on thousands of elderly patients, deceiving them into undergoing expensive, medically unnecessary tests and fraudulently billing the government for more than half a billion dollars," Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said. "Thanks to the outstanding work of our partners at the FBI, this defendant was brought back from overseas to face justice in the United States. Our message to fraudsters is clear: If you steal from American taxpayers and exploit vulnerable patients, we will find and prosecute you, no matter where you are."

Patel said Satary "exploited a program dedicated to helping our most vulnerable and instead stole for himself."

The FBI said Satary was apprehended overseas through coordination with U.S. and international law enforcement partners and was expected to land in the United States Tuesday morning. He was scheduled to make his initial appearance in federal court Tuesday afternoon. The case is being handled by the FBI's Miami Field Office.

MINNESOTA MAN MARKS FBI'S FIRST ARREST FROM DOJ'S 'MOST WANTED FRAUDSTERS' LIST

"This defendant is alleged to have engaged in a massive fraud scheme that preyed on elderly patients, duped them into taking expensive and unnecessary genetic tests, and billed the United States more than half a billion dollars," Assistant Attorney General Colin M. McDonald of the Justice Department's National Fraud Enforcement Division said. "As today's arrest shows, there is no safe haven for fraudsters who seek to exploit vulnerable Americans or our Nation's critical health care programs."

Patel also linked Satary's arrest to the Trump administration's broader effort to combat fraud against taxpayers.

"This is just the latest example showing President Trump's and Vice President Vance's White House Task Force to Eliminate Fraud will not be deterred in our mission to track down each and every fraudster who allegedly steals from American taxpayers," Patel said. "And it's yet another high-value target returned from overseas by this FBI — the 27th such transfer since June."

Satary's arrest marks the third capture since the FBI launched its Most Wanted Fraudsters list five weeks ago, an initiative aimed at locating and apprehending fugitives accused of large-scale financial crimes.

Last month, Said Abdullahi Ereg, a former Minneapolis grocery and deli owner wanted on federal charges since 2024, surrendered to federal authorities after being added to the list. Prosecutors allege Ereg fraudulently obtained more than $4.2 million from the Federal Child Nutrition Program during the COVID-19 pandemic.

A week later, the FBI arrested Herbert Leon Kimble, 60, in the Philippines after authorities said he spent nearly two years on the run. Kimble is accused of orchestrating a $1.2 billion Medicare fraud conspiracy.

item.title: Trump may have endorsed Darline Graham, but GOP Senate hopefuls aren’t backing down
item.description: Trump's endorsement of Darline Graham faces a major test as Russell Fry, Ralph Norman, and others jump into the South Carolina GOP Senate nomination race.
item.content:encoded:
As the starting gun in the Republican race to succeed the late Sen. Lindsey Graham of South Carolina is officially fired, the showdown is shaping up as the latest test of President Donald Trump's immense clout over the GOP and the power of his endorsements in party primaries.

On the eve of Tuesday's kickoff of a week-long filing period for the Aug. 11 primary, Sen. Darline Graham, who was appointed last week to fill the remainder of her late brother's term, announced in an interview on Fox News' Hannity that she's running for a full six-year term in the Senate.

Graham's announcement came three days after she met with Trump at the White House and the president gave her his "complete and total endorsement" as he encouraged her to run.

But Trump's backing of Graham, while extremely potent, isn't deterring other Republicans from jumping into the race.

WHO'S UP AND WHO'S DOWN: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS ON THE MIDTERM ELECTIONS

"South Carolina voters want to have a choice and not be told who they need to be voting for," Palmetto State-based Republican consultant Dave Wilson told Fox News Digital.

Veteran Republican strategist and communicator Ryan Williams noted that "the Trump endorsement is a major boost for any candidacy, but it doesn’t necessarily give you a lock on the seat."

"Senate seats in South Carolina do not come up very often and politics is all about timing. You’ve got to seize the opportunity when it presents itself," Williams told Fox News Digital. "If you’re an aspiring House member in South Carolina, this is your time to run regardless of whom the president is supporting."

Republican Rep. Ralph Norman, who unsuccessfully campaigned this year for the GOP gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster, announced his intention to run this past weekend.

WHAT DARLINE GRAHAM TOLD FOX NEWS' SEAN HANNITY

And on Monday, Republican Rep. Russell Fry jumped into the race.

Businessman Mark Lynch, who lost to Lindsey Graham by nearly 30 points in last month's GOP Senate primary, also announced he'd take another shot at the Republican nomination.

Republican Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette on Tuesday left the door open to running, writing in a social media post, "We need a strong, proven conservative to fill the shoes of a man who dedicated his life to serving the people and fighting for what South Carolina needs to support our businesses, our infrastructure, and our growing economy."

Wilson noted that "the president’s quick endorsement of Darline Graham was very symbolic."

But he added, "We’re seeing in South Carolina that his [the president’s] endorsement didn’t carry as much weight as people thought it was going to."

Wilson was referring to Trump’s endorsement of Evette ahead of the GOP gubernatorial primary. Evette was the top vote getter in the primary but lost the ensuing runoff election against South Carolina Attorney General Alan Wilson in a landslide. Days before the runoff, with Wilson leading the public opinion polls, Trump made an 11th-hour endorsement of the attorney general in addition to his backing of Evette.

"People here in South Carolina saw that Trump's endorsement of Evette didn't put her over the top, and they feel like the Senate primary could also be up for grabs," a Republican operative who asked for anonymity to speak more freely told Fox News Digital.

Trump a week ago lauded Fry, a two-term congressman who represents the northeastern corner of South Carolina, as "very talented" and "very popular," and said he would be an "outstanding" contender for the vacant Senate seat.

Asked about Fry's decision to launch a Senate campaign in the wake of Trump's backing of Darline Graham, a source in Fry's political orbit told Fox News Digital, "This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation."

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Fry and Norman, both Trump allies in the House, are highlighting their support for the president.

"South Carolina doesn't need just another senator. It needs President Trump's strongest ally in the United States Senate. Make no mistake: I will be President Trump’s favorite U.S. Senator, and you will have a MAGA fighter you can count on," Fry wrote in a social media post as he announced his Senate run.

Fry described himself as a "MAGA warrior" in an accompanying video, which included pictures of him smiling alongside Trump.

Norman emphasized that he's "running to represent the people of South Carolina in the U.S. Senate because we need a fighter who will stand with President Trump and carry on Lindsey Graham’s legacy!"

And pointing to Trump's top legislative priority, he highlighted, "I want to get the SAVE America Act passed in the Senate!"

Republican Sens. Mike Lee of Utah and Rick Scott of Florida, conservatives and staunch Trump allies in the chamber, both cited Norman's support for Trump's election reform bill as they went against the president and endorsed the congressman. Norman is also being backed by the political arm of the powerful conservative group, Turning Point USA.

Graham does have the backing of heavy hitters in the Senate GOP, however, including Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso, R-Wyo., who said that, "Darline Graham is an excellent senator — She’ll continue to be an excellent senator, and she has my full support."

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., however, is staying out of the race for now.

NANCY MACE REVEALS SENATE DECISION AS LINDSEY GRAHAM'S SISTER REDRAWS THE RACE

Thune said that Graham is a "strong candidate," and though she lacked elected experience, he noted she was "very accomplished in her own life.""She has a right, like everybody else, to get in that race," Thune said.

Backed with Trump's endorsement and enjoying plenty of name recognition thanks to her well-known last name, pundits view Darline Graham as a frontrunner in the three-week sprint to the Republican primary. And the winner of the GOP nomination will be considered the clear favorite against Democratic nominee Annie Andrews in the general election in the solidly red southeastern state.

Trump's endorsements aren't infallible, but his track record so far this cycle, albeit a couple of setbacks, is impressive.

But Williams noted, "There’s no downside for running. They’re all going to run as Trump Republicans, and they’re all going to flatter the president on the campaign trail."

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

A well-known House Republican who has clashed over the past year with Trump decided against a Senate run. Rep. Nancy Mace, who ran unsuccessfully this year for the GOP gubernatorial nomination, announced on Monday that she wouldn't launch a Senate campaign.

The filing period for the Senate primary ends at noon Eastern time next Tuesday, July 28.

If no candidate tops 50% in the Aug. 11 primary, a runoff election will be held among the top two voter-getters on August 25.

If no candidate reaches 50% of the vote, a runoff election would be held in late August.

Fox News' Tyler Olson contributed to this report.

item.title: The real reason behind Hegseth's viral military testosterone screening order
item.description: The Pentagon now requires annual testosterone screening for service members 30 and older, raising questions about treatment and fertility risks.
item.content:encoded:
Military veteran Chad Robichaux was home from Afghanistan, eating well and training hard enough to compete as a professional athlete. Still, something felt wrong.

Some days, the force recon Marine vet was irritable and agitated. Other days, he was deeply depressed. He was tired, struggled to focus and felt what he described as "a real sense of doom."

VA doctors prescribed antidepressants, he said. Only after seeking blood testing elsewhere did he learn his hormones were severely out of balance.

"Once I got my hormones in check, I didn’t need to be on SSRIs anymore," he said, referring to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor medication. "I felt like I knocked 10 to 15 years off the way I felt."

LOW TESTOSTERONE? HEGSETH SPARKS CONVERSATION AS DOCTORS REVEAL WARNING SIGNS EVERY MAN SHOULD KNOW

Now, the Pentagon is requiring every active-duty and reserve service member age 30 and older, man and woman, to be screened for testosterone deficiency each year — a sweeping new mandate shaped in part by veterans like Robichaux who say hormonal problems have gone undetected for years.

Service members younger than 30 will be permitted to request the screening as part of their annual periodic health assessment .

Secretary of War Pete Hegseth recently ordered the new protocol, saying it would establish a comprehensive baseline across the force and allow military doctors to offer "targeted testosterone therapy" when medically appropriate.

The order is expected to reach hundreds of thousands of troops, although the Pentagon has not said exactly how many people will be subject to mandatory screening.

More than 451,000 active-duty service members were age 31 or older in 2023, according to the department’s demographic data. That figure excludes 30-year-olds and members of the reserve components.

Roughly 82% of the active-duty force is male, but the directive also applies to women. The Pentagon did not immediately provide a breakdown of how many male and female service members will be screened.

The directive does not yet explain how testosterone deficiency will be defined in women, for whom diagnostic and treatment standards are less established.

It also is unclear whether testosterone deficiency is more common among service members than among similarly aged civilians. The Pentagon has not published a force-wide prevalence estimate, and Hegseth described the new screening program as a way to establish a comprehensive baseline across the military.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Pentagon for more details.

The Pentagon's new policy reflects a broader debate within medicine over whether symptoms such as fatigue, depression and cognitive decline are too often treated with medication before physicians rule out underlying physiological causes — including hormonal disorders — that can produce similar symptoms.

Testosterone testing was available to service members before Hegseth’s directive, but it was generally ordered when a physician believed a patient’s symptoms or medical history warranted it. It was not previously a required component of every annual periodic health assessment after age 30.

For Robichaux, the issue was personal.

"I’m kind of a grinding-through-things person," he told Fox News Digital. "I was trying to grind and grind through that state, and it was super frustrating because I’m like, ‘Man, what’s wrong with me?’"

That experience helped propel Robichaux into a yearslong campaign urging military and veterans’ health providers to look for hormonal deficiencies in troops experiencing fatigue, depression and other symptoms that can also signal mental health conditions.

Interviews with several people involved pushing policy makers for the screening requirement show how the mandate was promoted by an alliance of former special operators, members of Congress, physicians and private hormone-treatment executives — some of whom say the Pentagon has asked for their help implementing it.

Robichaux said his experience was not unusual. Through his work with the Mighty Oaks Foundation, which provides faith-based programs for veterans and service members, he encountered others reporting similar combinations of exhaustion, depression and diminished physical performance.

He came to believe that counseling and psychiatric treatment alone could not address every underlying problem.

"This shouldn’t be like an either-or," Robichaux said. "It should be an ‘and.’ We should bring them all together."

That conviction eventually brought together an unusual coalition on Capitol Hill.

'WOKE' MILITARY ACADEMIES TARGETED IN NEW BLUEPRINT FOR SWEEPING WARFIGHTING OVERHAUL

Hours before Hegseth announced the new screening requirement, Robichaux organized a forum in the Cannon House Office Building featuring veterans, lawmakers, physicians and private-sector health executives.

Rep. Eli Crane, R-Ariz., a former Navy SEAL, agreed to serve as its congressional host. Crane said he first became aware of the issue around the time he left the Navy in 2014, when other SEALs told him that doctors had detected low testosterone among members of the special operations community.

"It actually really surprised me. I thought it would be the exact opposite," Crane said.

SLEEP APNEA MAY BE QUIETLY CHANGING YOUR BODY IN AN UNEXPECTED WAY, STUDY FINDS

They attributed the problem to the chronic stress, physical demands and sleep deprivation associated with their work, he recalled. But at the time, Crane said, service members struggled to access testing and treatment.

"I think this is a tremendous step forward for our men and women in the service, whether they’re a part of special operations or not," he said.

Testosterone generally declines with age, but low levels can also result from obesity, diabetes, testicular injury, pituitary disorders and medications including opioids and corticosteroids. Severe illness, inadequate nutrition and excessive exercise can temporarily lower a reading as well.

Some of those risk factors may be especially relevant in a military population exposed to demanding training, irregular sleep, injuries and prolonged stress.

Also participating in the effort was Sidney Gordon, CEO of Core Medical Group, a private clinic specializing in hormone replacement therapy. Gordon said he has spent roughly a decade pushing military and veterans’ health providers to more closely examine hormonal deficiencies.

His affiliated nonprofit, Core Medical Foundation, has paid for veterans to receive hormone replacement therapy, Gordon said. His for-profit clinic also treats veterans, active-duty service members, first responders and civilians.

Gordon argued that military and VA providers have too often prescribed SSRIs for symptoms such as fatigue, depression and difficulty concentrating without first conducting sufficiently broad hormonal testing.

But Gordon and the other advocates stressed that the goal is not to place every service member on testosterone.

Dr. Gerry Stanley, a family physician and advisor to Core Medical, said physicians must evaluate a patient’s symptoms alongside multiple laboratory markers and consider other potential causes of a low reading.

"One of the axioms of medicine is you treat people, not labs," Stanley said.

Some service members may ultimately require testosterone, he said, while others could benefit from improved sleep, reduced stress, lifestyle changes or treatments that help the body produce more of the hormone naturally.

"This isn’t cookie cutter," Stanley said. "It’s not that everybody we’re talking about gets testosterone."

Under widely accepted medical guidelines, one low result is not sufficient to diagnose testosterone deficiency. Physicians generally seek at least two early-morning readings, evaluate the patient’s symptoms and investigate whether the result could be connected to medication, illness, obesity, sleep or another potentially reversible cause.

A low result would also not automatically mean testosterone shots. Depending on the cause, treatment could involve lifestyle changes, addressing an underlying condition or adjusting another medication. Testosterone replacement, when medically appropriate, can be administered through injections, gels, patches or other formulations.

The Pentagon has said military doctors will be able to provide "targeted testosterone therapy," but has not detailed which treatments will be covered or whether service members will receive fertility counseling before beginning therapy.

The advocates’ involvement did not end with the announcement.

Robichaux said he, Gordon, Stanley and other participants met with Pentagon officials and were asked to help connect the department with physicians and industry leaders as it develops the screening and treatment protocols.

The screening itself is a blood test, but hormone specialists caution that treatment following a low result can carry significant consequences.

The broad mandate has drawn caution from the Endocrine Society, which represents physicians and researchers specializing in hormonal conditions.

"There is insufficient evidence to support a general recommendation to perform population-level screening for hypogonadism in asymptomatic men," the organization said in a statement responding to the Pentagon initiative.

The society said a diagnosis should require symptoms and consistently low readings from at least two early-morning, fasting tests. It also stressed that potentially reversible contributors—including obesity, opioid use and corticosteroids—should be considered before testosterone is prescribed.

For male service members, one of the most immediate is fertility. Taking external testosterone can sharply reduce sperm production, sometimes suppressing it entirely. The Endocrine Society recommends against prescribing testosterone therapy to men planning to have children in the near term.

The Endocrine Society also responded to the Pentagon announcement by saying there is insufficient evidence to support population-wide screening of men without symptoms. The organization warned that testosterone measurements can vary by time of day, laboratory method, sleep, illness and other factors, creating a risk of both overdiagnosis and missed diagnoses.

The advocates involved in the Pentagon push said they agree that screening should not automatically lead to treatment.

"We’re not asking to give guys testosterone," Robichaux said. "What we’re asking to do is, before you give them a handful of SSRI pills, give them a blood panel."

Women also can experience hormonal disruption from prolonged stress, intense training and inadequate nutrition, though it does not present as a direct equivalent of testosterone deficiency in men.

Those conditions can interfere with ovulation and reduce estrogen and progesterone, potentially affecting menstrual cycles, fertility and bone density. The Pentagon has not yet detailed how the new screening program will account for those broader hormonal issues in female troops.

Perimenopause is generally evaluated through symptoms and menstrual changes rather than routine hormone testing. TRICARE covers FDA-approved hormone therapy when medically appropriate.

The details of the new directive will now fall to Pentagon health officials. Hegseth directed the department to update its policies by Aug. 15, make testing available across the Military Health System and establish an advisory council of outside experts to guide implementation.

Those guidelines will determine how an initial low result is confirmed, when treatment is appropriate and whether service members receive fertility counseling before beginning testosterone therapy.

item.title: Texas Gov Abbott signs executive order cracking down on 'birth tourism,’ says US citizenship not for sale
item.description: Greg Abbott's executive order empowers Texas agencies to investigate hospitals and providers accused of advertising birth tourism packages.
item.content:encoded:
Texas Gov. Greg Abbott on Tuesday took steps to crack down on the birth tourism industry in the state in an effort to curb the flow of pregnant women coming into the United States with the intention of giving birth so their child can automatically obtain American citizenship.

An executive order signed by Abbott empowers state agencies to investigate birth tourism schemes within Texas’ healthcare industry and to take enforcement action against any licensed or regulated individual or entity found to be advertising, soliciting, assisting or providing unlawful birth tourism schemes.

"Texas will not tolerate the exploitation of our immigration laws by individuals traveling to the U.S. illegally or under false pretenses to give birth and secure citizenship for their child," Abbott said in a statement. "This executive order directs state agencies to investigate birth tourism schemes and hold accountable any provider that facilitates this fraud."

BIRTH TOURISM CRACKDOWN EXPANDS AS HOUSE CHAIRMAN RAISES CRIMINAL CONSPIRACY CASE

"U.S. citizenship is not for sale, and Texas will put a stop to individuals seeking to profit from birth tourism," the governor added.

Enforcement action includes revoking or suspending licenses, prohibiting participation in state contracts, denying state benefits, or pursuing other enforcement actions.

The executive order builds on a previous directive from Abbott when he tasked the state Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC) earlier this month to investigate Texas hospitals accused of advertising birth tourism packages.

SEN ERIC SCHMITT SEEKS TO END BIRTH TOURISM, REVOKE CITIZENSHIP FOR BABIES OF FOREIGN DIPLOMATS

One hospital, Mission Regional Medical Center, previously confirmed to Fox News Digital that it advertised Spanish-language "Birth Packages in South Texas" on billboards in Mexico. The ads promoted childbirth services to pregnant foreign nationals near the U.S.-Mexico border. The services included promotional deliveries starting at $3,950 for a natural birth and $5,525 for a C-section.

"The marketing materials regarding maternity services are no longer in use due to any unintended misunderstanding," a hospital spokesperson said in a statement to Fox News at the time. "We do not support or facilitate any unlawful activity and work to comply with all applicable federal and state laws and regulations."

Opponents of birthright citizenship have long cited birth tourism as a major immigration loophole.

Meanwhile, the State Department has enacted several measures to crack down on birth tourism through amended visa visitor rules and giving consular officers the power to deny visa applications if they believe the applicant's primary purpose is to give birth in the U.S.

President Donald Trump has called birth tourism a major abuse of the immigration system and a magnet for illegal immigration.

item.title: Caitlin Clark keeps delivering record WNBA viewership as Fever top 1 million viewers yet again
item.description: Caitlin Clark and the Indiana Fever broke USA Sports' WNBA viewership record for the second straight week, averaging 1.14 million viewers.
item.content:encoded:
Caitlin Clark and the Indiana Fever’s July 15 game against the Golden State Valkyries averaged 1.14 million viewers, according to USA Sports PR.

It was the most-watched WNBA game in USA Sports’ WNBA history, beating the record that Clark and the Fever set... one week earlier.

In fact, this is the third time that Clark and the Fever averaged at least one million viewers on USA Sports, starting with their June 16 game against the Toronto Tempo. That was the USA Sports record until the team broke it on July 8.

WNBA WANTS A BIGGER STORY THAN CAITLIN CLARK, BUT TV RATINGS KEEP POINTING BACK TO HER

The July 8 game against the Los Angeles Sparks was particularly impressive because it averaged 1.04 million viewers across USA Network and CNBC despite tipping off at 10 p.m. ET on a Wednesday. It became the first WNBA game with a 10 p.m. ET start to reach one million viewers.

Apparently, that record wasn’t good enough.

The Fever-Valkyries audience was up 172% compared to the 2025 WNBA cable average. USA Sports also said it was the second-most-watched WNBA game on cable or streaming this season and the top cable entertainment program of the night.

The only WNBA game on cable or streaming to draw more viewers this season was another Fever game. Indiana’s July 5 matchup against the Las Vegas Aces averaged 1.55 million viewers on ESPN, even though neither Clark nor A’ja Wilson played.

That number has been used by some as evidence that the Fever’s television appeal has moved beyond Clark.

It has, to a degree. But it didn’t happen by accident.

The Fever can draw viewers without Clark now because Clark turned them into a national television team in the first place. Indiana wasn’t producing seven-figure cable audiences before she arrived. Not even close.

The attention Clark draws is still tied to production, not just her presence. The WNBA named Clark Eastern Conference Player of the Week after she averaged 25.0 points, 2.3 rebounds and 7.7 assists.

That stretch included a 45-point, 10-assist performance in a win over the Seattle Storm, making Clark the first player in WNBA history with at least 40 points and 10 assists in a game.

WNBA ALL-STAR CHARITY INITIATIVE SUPPORTS SPORTS GROUP THAT SAYS IT IS ‘MOVING BEYOND THE BINARY OF GIRL’

And Clark was very much on the court for the WNBA’s biggest television number of the season.

Three days before the Golden State game, the Fever’s 109-75 victory over the Aces averaged 2.64 million viewers across NBC and Peacock. The audience peaked at 2.8 million and made it the most-watched WNBA game of 2026. It was also the league’s most-watched regular-season Sunday game since 1998 and its second-most-watched regular-season game since 2000.

NBC/Peacock and USA Network don't have the same reach, so the two audiences are not directly comparable. But they don't need to be.

The Fever delivered the WNBA’s biggest overall audience of the season on Sunday. Three nights later, they broke the USA Sports record.

The Golden State game wasn’t especially memorable for Indiana, either.

The Fever lost, 88-75, while Clark finished with 13 points on 4-of-14 shooting and committed four turnovers. She also played through a leg contusion suffered during the game.

More than 1.1 million people watched anyway.

ANGEL REESE’S LATEST WNBA CONTROVERSY SHOWS HOW VICTIMHOOD BECAME CENTRAL TO HER BRAND

Before Clark entered the league, the WNBA had gone nearly 16 years without a game averaging one million viewers.

USA Sports just waited seven days for Clark to draw one million viewers for the third time this season on its network.

It will get another chance soon. The Fever host the Connecticut Sun on Wednesday on USA Network, giving Clark and Indiana a chance to break the network's WNBA record for the third straight week.

item.title: New Jersey removes 6,600 people from voter rolls who were eligible to cast a ballot for 2024 election
item.description: New Jersey announced that it removed 6,600 noncitizens from its voter rolls who were registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024.
item.content:encoded:
New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced Tuesday that the state has removed roughly 6,600 noncitizens from its voter rolls who would have been eligible to vote in the 2024 presidential election.

Sherrill, a Democrat, said there was a "serious software error" in New Jersey's Motor Vehicle System that led to thousands of people without legal status being registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024.

"In the case of this error, some individuals indicated that they weren't citizens and weren't eligible to vote, but the software registered them anyway. The error in the software was corrected a year later in June of 2024," Sherrill said in a press conference.

According to the state's preliminary findings, fewer than 400 individuals who were improperly registered ended up voting. Sherill said they lived all across the state and mostly lacked a party affiliation, though there were some registered as Democrats or Republicans.

MORE THAN A QUARTER-MILLION NONCITIZENS MAY BE REGISTERED TO VOTE IN 4 KEY STATES, DHS ALLEGES

Sherill said that immediately upon learning this last Wednesday, she ordered an immediate investigation and the removal of anyone who was not supposed to be on the state's voter rolls.

She added that the state's Division of Elections is currently working to remove ineligible voters.

"I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time. This failure didn’t occur under my watch, but accountability starts now. I am taking action to prevent anything like it from occurring in the future and make sure those responsible are held accountable," Sherrill said in a statement on social media.

SEN MARK WARNER CALLED OUT IN ABC INTERVIEW FOR INACCURATE VOTER ID CLAIMS

She also attacked President Donald Trump for working to "undermine Americans' faith in our elections by spreading lies about legitimate election results, attempting to overturn a free and fair election, encouraging an attack on our Capitol when he couldn't accept the will of the voters, and defunding election security at the federal level."

This comes after Trump announced last week that the Department of Homeland Security found that 250,000 noncitizens are registered to vote in New Jersey, California, Nevada and Pennsylvania.

In the four letters dated Friday, DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin said the department compared publicly available voter registration records with federal immigration data and uncovered thousands of potential matches. He asked state election officials to work with DHS to verify the identities before taking any action.

According to the letters, DHS estimates there may be as many as 190,832 noncitizens registered to vote in California, 35,152 in New Jersey, 15,903 in Nevada and 14,576 in Pennsylvania. 

The department said it found 81,336 California registrants, 19,497 New Jersey registrants, 8,576 Nevada registrants and 8,594 Pennsylvania registrants whose names, dates of birth, addresses and Social Security numbers match noncitizens in DHS records.

DHS has described these findings as a preliminary review.

item.title: New York City landlords warn American dream is slipping away under socialist rent policies
item.description: New York landlords push back against Democratic Socialists of America rent freeze calls, saying property taxes, insurance and mortgages erode profits.
item.content:encoded:
Democratic Socialists' calls to freeze rents and reject double-digit investment returns for landlords are fueling fears among some New York City property owners, who say years of rising costs and restrictive housing policies are pushing family-run businesses to the brink.

"It shows how flawed the system is in its entirety and how inefficient everything is here," New York landlord Jose Tur told Fox News Tuesday.

Tur, whose late grandfather spent decades building the family's New York real estate business after emigrating from Cuba, said the debate over landlord profits overlooks the hardships facing many small property owners.

NYC RENT BOARD DISSENTER WARNS MAMDANI-BACKED FREEZE COULD HURT AFFORDABLE HOUSING OVER TIME: 'SLOW BURN'

He recalled how his grandfather, who purchased his first apartment building in 1979, spent decades growing the family business before he was reportedly mugged at gunpoint by a tenant who had not paid rent in five years. Tur said his grandfather suffered a stroke and died two weeks later at age 99.

Fellow New York landlord Natalia Bonanno also relayed her family's struggle amid what she characterized as growing attacks from the far left: mounting operating costs that have made it increasingly difficult to keep rent-stabilized buildings financially viable.

She told "America's Newsroom" that her father came to the U.S. as an immigrant with a dream. He became the first in his family to earn a college degree and later became an engineer before deciding he wanted to own property.

"He started to invest also in the '70s and buying properties in Brooklyn and then passed them down to my sister and I," Bonanno recalled.

Today, Bonanno said that dream is becoming increasingly difficult to sustain. She disputed claims that landlords are routinely earning double-digit returns, arguing such figures ignore the rising costs of mortgages, insurance, taxes and the expense of maintaining aging rent-stabilized buildings, many of which were constructed in the early 20th century.

STEVE FORBES: MAMDANI’S SOCIALIST RENT-CONTROL PUTS NEW YORK ON THE ROAD TO HOUSING RUIN

"I don't know where they're getting 12% from," Bonanno said, responding to remarks made Friday by New York City Democratic Socialists of America co-Chair Gustavo Gordillo.

"I know a lot of people refer to the NOI figure that came out recently, and that doesn't take into account our mortgage and insurance," she added.

Gordillo, speaking to "The Story" host Martha MacCallum last week, argued that landlords should not expect double-digit investment returns and dismissed claims that rent freezes would drive property owners out of business.

Tur also disputed Gordillo's comments, arguing they were not made in reference to rent-stabilized properties, which he and Bonanno both own.

When asked whether they planned to remain in the business, Tur said he hopes to continue the work his grandfather spent decades building, describing it as a responsibility to both his family and the tenants he has known for years.

"I think it's the right thing to do on behalf of my family and what my grandfather built and also on behalf of the tenants," Tur said.

"These are people that we've known for several years. It's not like we're a huge company that's not present. We're boots on the ground."

Bonanno, however, said her family's future in New York is far less certain.

"We're selling most of our rent-stabilized buildings because we just don't see a future here for having profit," she said.

"We hope we could stay in New York City and maybe invest in non-rent-stabilized properties, but if not, we'll have to just sell and purchase properties in other states."

item.title: Journey star Jonathan Cain demands Bruce Springsteen 'shut up,' act like he was born in the USA
item.description: Journey keyboardist Jonathan Cain urged Bruce Springsteen to "shut up" about politics on stage, calling the rocker an "annoying, bitter old man."
item.content:encoded:
Journey keyboardist Jonathan Cain didn’t hold back his feelings about Bruce Springsteen during a podcast appearance, telling the rock icon to "shut up" about politics on stage.

Speaking on the "Tom Barnard" podcast, Cain called out stars like Springsteen and actor Robert De Niro for using their art as political platforms. He argued that musicians should stick to entertaining fans, not telling them how to vote.

"I used to be a Democrat back in the old days, but I just couldn’t go there anymore, you know?" Cain said in comments from May, resurfaced by Variety.

DWAYNE 'THE ROCK' JOHNSON BRANDED A COWARD BY HOLLYWOOD STARS AFTER REFUSING TO SPEAK OUT ON POLITICS

Calling himself a "diehard conservative," Cain called on his fellow performers to stick with what they’re good at.

"Guys like Bruce Springsteen should shut up, you know?" Cain said, adding, "Him and, who’s the other one? Robert De Niro. I mean, who cares, guys? Really? Do your art. I try to keep politics out of my music."

Cain went even further, directly addressing "The Boss" and his iconic 1984 anthem, "Born in the U.S.A."

BRUCE SPRINGSTEEN TRASHED BY HOME STATE PAPER OVER EXPENSIVE ANTI-TRUMP CONCERTS

"And Bruce, I mean, what the heck? I used to like him, and now he’s just annoying. He’s an annoying, bitter old man. I mean, stop it. Really. You were born in the U.S.A., weren’t you, dude? So act like it. But even that song, if you look at the lyrics, I mean, it’s a kind of a diss to our country," he said.

Springsteen has frequently used his concerts and public appearances to speak out against the Trump administration and conservative policies. During a January concert in New Jersey, he quoted Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey, telling the crowd that "ICE should get the f--- out of Minneapolis."

During a PBS special titled "Bruce Springsteen: Finding America in Song," the singer defended his political commentary as patriotism.

BRUCE SPRINGSTEEN OFFERS ‘PRAYER’ FOR TRUMP AFTER WHCA DINNER SHOOTING DESPITE MONTHS OF CRITICISM

"I believe in critical patriotism," Springsteen said. "I believe that’s the definition of a patriot, you know, that you love your country so much that you are willing to look at it clearly, recognize its faults, encourage it to be a better place, and believe that you carry in your heart the country that is waiting."

Cain, who is married to a senior advisor for Donald Trump, strongly disagreed with that philosophy. He argued that few musicians are truly releasing patriotic music anymore, pointing to himself and Kid Rock as rare exceptions.

"Me and Kid Rock, we’re probably on an island," Cain said. "There’s a few of us, and I proudly stand there with my flag on that island. And if anybody’s got a problem with me, America and Jesus, then bring it."

Kid Rock has similarly come out against Springsteen, calling him "just another person with TDS," the acronym for "Trump Derangement Syndrome."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"To be in Europe talking junk about our president who gets up and works his ass off for this country, every day, and his administration is doing such great things… Thank God for him. But to do that in Europe… what a punk move," Rock told "Fox & Friends" in May after Springsteen said the U.S. was in the hands of a "corrupt, incompetent, and treasonous administration" while in Berlin.

Cain added that while his Christian faith is central to his life, he deliberately chooses not to lecture his audiences during Journey concerts, preferring to bring people together.

"Now, people say, ‘Well, you’re a Christian, and you’re conservative,’ and I’m like, ‘So what?’ I don’t preach it on stage. I don’t spend 10 minutes talking about it," Cain said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Springsteen's representatives for comment but did not immediately hear back.

item.title: Republican Rep. María Elvira Salazar declares, 'After Cuba, Nicaragua is next'
item.description: Criticizing Nicaragua's Daniel Ortega, U.S. Rep. María Elvira Salazar, R-Fla., said in a post on X that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next."
item.content:encoded:
Republican Rep. María Elvira Salazar of Florida stated in a Monday social media post that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next." 

The Central American nation's president, Daniel Ortega, said on Sunday, "There won't be any more elections here for them to try to seize the government and seize power," according to Reuters. "The days when parties backed by the Yankees (United States) and the Somocistas would return to power are over - never again," he said, according to the outlet.

In her Monday post on X, Salazar said, "Daniel Ortega just announced that Nicaragua will no longer hold elections. When a communist dictator knows the people would vote him out, he takes away their vote. Repression, fear, and force become the only way to stay in power. The Ortega-Murillo regime has declared war on the Nicaraguan people’s right to choose their own future."

Ortega's wife Rosario Murillo is co-president, reports indicate.

"The United States must stand unequivocally with the people of Nicaragua, increase the pressure on this despicable regime, and ensure that those responsible for its crimes are held accountable. After Cuba, Nicaragua is next," Salazar added in the post.

NICARAGUA'S ORTEGA SAYS COUNTRY WILL ‘NEVER AGAIN’ HOLD ELECTIONS, SHUTTING OUT OPPOSITION 

The U.S. snatched Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro and his wife out of Venezuela and transported them back to the U.S. earlier this year. 

The U.S. later launched a war against the Islamic Republic of Iran in conjunction with Israel.

And then during a speech in March, U.S. President Donald Trump said "Cuba's next."

US MILITARY HITS IRAN IN PUSH TO DEGRADE REGIME'S ABILITY TO TARGET SHIPS IN STRAIT OF HORMUZ

Fox News Digital reached out to Salazar's office on Tuesday to ask more about her comment suggesting that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next."

The congresswoman said in a Tuesday post on X, "One by one, the dictators of the last era are answering for their crimes. Maduro sleeps in a cell in New York. The Ayatollah is dead. Raúl Castro is under indictment. Daniel Ortega, whose regime killed more than 300 peaceful protesters in 2018, jailed opposition candidates before elections, exiled priests, stripped hundreds of Nicaraguans of their citizenship, and has now abolished elections altogether, still sits in Managua. Communist dictators don't fear losing elections. They fear having them. His day will come." 

U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said in a Tuesday statement, "Daniel Ortega’s declaration that under his family’s dictatorship Nicaragua will never again hold elections lays bare their true authoritarian nature. Daniel Ortega and Rosario Murillo have abandoned even the pretense of popular consent.

CUBA BUILT COVERT NETWORK INSIDE AMERICA THROUGH SPIES, ACTIVISTS AND UNIVERSITIES, STATE DEPT ALLEGES

"The Nicaraguan people have the right to choose their own leaders through democratic elections. The United States calls on the international community to join forces to demonstrate to the Murillo-Ortega dictatorship that it cannot expect to maintain business as usual with other nations when it is thwarting the basic tenets of our democratic hemisphere," Rubio asserted.

item.title: Bill Maher recounts Larry David falling out, celebrity backlash after Trump White House dinner
item.description: Larry David penned a New York Times essay titled, "My Dinner With Adolf," after Bill Maher's White House dinner with President Donald Trump, and their friendship has yet to recover.
item.content:encoded:
Comedian and talk show host Bill Maher recounted his falling out with Larry David on Monday after meeting with President Donald Trump at the White House for a dinner last year.

In an interview with The New York Times Magazine, Maher discussed how David was "so outraged" by his meeting with Trump that he penned an essay in The New York Times titled, "My Dinner With Adolf," adding that the two men’s relationship had yet to recover.

Jerry Seinfeld also reportedly weighed in, adding both Maher and David "both got kind of bugged out," following the White House dinner, which was organized by Kid Rock.

PATTON OSWALT SAYS BILL MAHER WAS WRONG TO TRY TO 'PLACATE' TRUMP, ARGUES IT ALWAYS BACKFIRES

Maher initially viewed his meeting with Trump as an attempt to infiltrate the president’s inner circle.

"It’s an administration of ‘people are saying,’" Maher told NYT Magazine. "If that’s how the president gets his information, then be one of the people saying things."

However, just 12 days after the meeting with Trump, Maher appeared on his HBO show "Real Time" to offer words of praise for the president — a move that shocked his personal friends.

BILL MAHER ADMITS HE ENJOYS WATCHING TRUMP GET COMBATIVE WITH JOURNALISTS IN TENSE INTERVIEWS

Maher told viewers Trump was both "gracious and measured," while adding the president was more down-to-earth in private than the "crazy person" he seems to be in public.

However, Maher added that "it doesn’t matter who he is in a private dinner with a comedian — it matters who he is on the world stage."

His generous description of Trump reportedly ruffled feathers within his inner circle, such as actor Sean Penn, who "didn’t understand" Maher’s emphasis on the president’s likability.

THE FEUD BETWEEN TRUMP AND MAHER IS HEATING UP AGAIN AS HBO HOST RESPONDS TO THE PRESIDENT’S LATEST DIG

The uncharacteristic words of praise from Maher were short-lived, however, with the podcast host calling Trump’s first 100 days back in office "probably 100 things to legitimately hate."

Earlier this year, Trump declared the dinner to be a "total waste of time," while labeling Maher a "jerk and a "highly overrated lightweight." Maher also touched on his perceived annoyance of other high-profile comedians, following several spars with celebrities on the national stage.

After Maher reportedly blasted comedian Dave Chappelle for his comments directed at free speech at the 2025 Riyadh Comedy Festival, Chappelle offered some harsh words for the host.

"I’m so f------ tired of his little smug, cracker-a-- commentary," Chappelle reportedly said in his Netflix special, in response to Maher’s criticism.

JONATHAN TURLEY: TRUMP’S KENNEDY CENTER NAME CHANGE WILL KEEP LAWYERS BUSY FOR YEARS

However, not all of Maher’s colleagues reportedly offered harsh criticisms.

Louis C.K. previously referred to Maher as "allergic to bulls---," adding that he "doesn’t need you to think he’s a good guy — but he is," according to the Times.

Fox News Digital reached out to Maher and David for comment but did not immediately receive a response.

item.title: Christian lawmaker says UK barred her from entering country after Finland 'hate speech' conviction
item.description: Finnish lawmaker Päivi Räsänen says her U.K. travel authorization was canceled after disclosing her hate speech conviction over biblical views.
item.content:encoded:
A Finnish Christian lawmaker says British authorities revoked her permission to travel to the United Kingdom following her recent "hate speech" conviction over statements expressing biblical views on marriage and sexuality, raising fresh concerns among religious freedom advocates about the consequences of the ruling.

Päivi Räsänen, a member of Finland's Parliament and the country's former interior minister, said her previously approved U.K. Electronic Travel Authorization was canceled without explanation, jeopardizing plans to speak at events in Northern Ireland next month. Räsänen is appealing her conviction to the European Court of Human Rights.

A screenshot of the cancellation message shared with Fox News Digital read, "Dear ETA Customer, The information you provided in your ETA application suggests that you are not eligible for an ETA and it has been cancelled. If you still want to travel to the UK, you will need to apply or reapply for one of the following: Electronic Travel Authorisation Standard visitor visa Transit visa Temporary work – creative worker visa."

HASAN PIKER SAYS UK HAS BARRED HIM, TRASHES 'UNBELIEVABLE...POWER' OF PRO-ISRAEL GROUPS

"I tried to apply for an Electronic Travel Authorization (ETA) from the United Kingdom authorities because of my future travels to UK," Räsänen told Fox News Digital in a statement.

"As part of the application process, I was asked to disclose any criminal convictions within the past 12 months. At first, I got a positive answer on 17th June, but on 3rd July I got a shocking message from GOV.UK that my application was cancelled, ‘The information you provided in your ETA application suggests that you are not eligible for an ETA and it has been cancelled.’"

According to Alliance Defending Freedom, who has been representing Räsänen in litigation, in 2019 she criticized her church's sponsorship of an LGBT event in a post on X that included a passage from Romans 1. Finnish prosecutors later charged her with "agitation against a minority group" under the country's criminal code, citing the social media post, a 2004 church pamphlet she wrote about marriage and sexuality, and remarks she made during a 2019 radio discussion.

BIBLE VERSE LEADS TO TRIAL IN EUROPE. GROWING CRACKDOWN THREATENS OUR US-UK VALUES

Räsänen told Fox News Digital Monday that she had planned to travel to Northern Ireland in August to speak at the Bangor Worldwide Missionary Convention about free speech and faith. She also said the cancellation has already affected her travel plans, forcing her to reroute a recent return trip from California through Dallas rather than connecting through London's Heathrow Airport.

"On 26 March 2026, I was convicted by a narrow 3–2 majority decision of the Supreme Court over statements expressing biblical views on marriage and sexuality in a booklet I wrote in 2004," Räsänen said.

Her booklet is called, "Male and Female He Created Them," and Räsänen said she was sentenced to a total of 20 days' wages worth of fines.

Räsänen said she plans to challenge the decision before the European Court of Human Rights, arguing authorities failed to properly consider her rights to free speech and religious freedom under the European Convention on Human Rights. She said the cancellation has had broader consequences than she anticipated, affecting not only her ability to express her views but also complicating international travel and participation in conferences.

"My greatest concern is that this decision has left many people with uncertainty, confusion, and fear about the consequences of exercising their freedom of speech and faith in a peaceful manner, and about where the line lies between lawful and prohibited speech," Räsänen said.

"Also Lutheran Bishop Juhana Pohjola, who was convicted alongside me because of publishing my booklet, has been denied the travel authorization ETA," she added. "However, I trust that all of this is in God’s hands, and I pray that He leads and guides in the way He wills. During these seven years, I have witnessed how the Lord miraculously turns evil things into good."

Räsänen said she has longstanding ties to the United Kingdom through her parliamentary work, including membership in the Finland–United Kingdom friendship group and previous visits as both a member of Parliament and Finland's interior minister.

She said she plans to travel to the U.K. again for official duties and currently serves on Finland's Foreign Affairs Committee, the country's delegation to the NATO Parliamentary Assembly and the board of its Inter-Parliamentary Union group.

GOP LAWMAKERS URGE SUPREME COURT TO STRENGTHEN FIRST AMENDMENT PROTECTIONS FOR STREET PREACHERS

Räsänen added that she has contacted the U.K. Home Office and written to Shabana Mahmood, secretary of state for the Home Department, seeking a response, but has not yet received one.

In a statement to Fox News Digital, Lorcan Price, senior legal counsel at Alliance Defending Freedom said, "The refusal of a travel authorization for Päivi Räsänen is the latest example of the United Kingdom government’s authoritarian attempt to suppress any speech or speaker they disagree with."

Price added, "The fact that Päivi was due to address a conference on the theme of religious freedom makes this all the more ironic and outrageous. The UK government has shown contempt for freedom of expression, and this is just the latest example."

Fox News Digital reached out to the U.K. Home Office and Mahmood for comment.

item.title: Top Dem hopeful flips script after years in socialist orbit with puzzling claim
item.description: Abdul El-Sayed attended six DSA events and headlined a fundraiser, yet insists he believes in capitalism ahead of the Michigan Senate primary.
item.content:encoded:
Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed has a long history of attending events hosted by the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) despite his insistence that he is not a socialist.

Between 2017 and 2020, El-Sayed attended and spoke at six events held by the DSA, including a 2019 fundraiser which he headlined. Despite the longstanding relationship, El-Sayed has distanced himself from the DSA when interviewed by the press and outright rejected being labeled as a "socialist."

"I'm not technically or practically DSA," El-Sayed told the Wall Street Journal earlier this month. In an interview with CNN, he similarly stated that "I am not somebody who is a socialist. I believe in capitalism, I just believe that capitalism has to be regulated."

FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS: VOTERS SAY THEY’RE IN ECONOMIC PAIN, BUT WILL DEMOCRATS GAIN?

While El-Sayed has made a point to separate himself from the DSA, the organization made it clear that the two were close.

At the 2019 event, which raised $10,000 for the organization, El-Sayed was described by DSA organizers as "a longtime friend of Detroit DSA." He was photographed smiling next to Rep. Rashida Tlaib, a self-described socialist, behind a table with a poster on it depicting a hand labeled "DSA" choking a snake labeled "capitalism."

El-Sayed’s first recorded appearance at a DSA event was during his run for Michigan governor in 2017. Other DSA events he spoke at focused on Medicare for All and the Green New Deal.

BERNIE SANDERS, DSA REVEAL DEMANDS FOR DEM PARTY AFTER SOCIALISTS SWEEP NEW YORK ELECTIONS

The Washington Free Beacon first reported on El-Sayed’s attendance at the DSA events.

El-Sayed faces Rep. Haley Stevens, a moderate, in the state’s Democratic Senate primary. Some have speculated that his efforts to distance himself from the DSA are part of a strategy to appeal to Michigan’s relatively evenly split electorate.

In addition to his years-long relationship with the DSA, self-described socialists have also thrown their support behind El-Sayed.

SOCIALIST-BACKED DEM WHO RIPPED THE WEALTHY CRACKS STATE’S TOP 1%, TAX RETURNS SHOW

Sen. Bernie Sanders, for instance, endorsed El-Sayed on the same day he launched his campaign for Michigan’s Senate seat. The Vermont senator also endorsed El-Sayed’s ill-fated gubernatorial bid.

El-Sayed explained at greater length in an interview with Politico that, while he agrees with many of the policies put forward by DSA-aligned politicians, he remains a capitalist at heart.

"I don’t have to be a socialist to think that big corporations shouldn’t be able to nickel and dime us at every turn. I don’t have to be a socialist to believe that everybody in the richest, most powerful country in the world can have guaranteed health insurance," he told reporter Dasha Burns. "​​Like again, maybe I’m just making the mistake of trying to be nuanced in our politics, but I believe that we can have healthcare, that we can regulate on the biggest corporations in the world, that we can make it easier for you to make your first million rather than for Elon Musk to make a trillion. And that doesn’t make me a socialist. That just makes me somebody who wants government to work for people."

El-Sayed himself very well could be a millionaire, with recently released tax returns showing that he and his wife collectively earned $686,069 in 2025. The Senate hopeful also has an expansive collection of luxury watches.

The El-Sayed campaign did not respond to a request for comment when reached by Fox News Digital on Tuesday.

item.title: NYC Legionnaires' disease outbreak kills fourth victim, 76 cases reported
item.description: A fourth person has died as 76 Legionnaires' disease cases are now linked to cooling towers in Upper East Side buildings, including the Guggenheim Museum.
item.content:encoded:
An outbreak of Legionnaires' disease in New York City killed a fourth person as 76 cases have now been reported in the city, city health authorities announced Monday.

The outbreak, which city authorities have linked to a series of cooling towers in Upper East Side buildings, has hospitalized 60 people, 53 of whom were discharged while seven remained in the hospital, according to authorities.

The fourth death announced Monday follows a first death from the disease on Friday. City officials announced two additional deaths over the weekend.

Legionnaires disease comes from live Legionella bacteria infecting a person's lungs. While, according to New York City health authorities, most people exposed to the bacteria don't get sick, infection can cause a severe form of pneumonia that can be fatal.

DEADLY LEGIONNAIRES' DISEASE OUTBREAK SPARKS CONCERN IN MAJOR US CITY: KNOW THE SYMPTOMS

"Symptoms typically develop two to fourteen days after exposure and include fever, chills, muscle aches, shortness of breath, and cough. Legionnaires’ disease can be effectively treated with antibiotics when diagnosed early," New York City's Health Department wrote in a statement.

City officials discovered the recent outbreak on July 2, and quickly tied it to a series of cooling towers in 31 different buildings in the city. 

NYC HEALTH OFFICIALS WARN CENTRAL PARK VISITORS AFTER LEGIONNAIRES’ OUTBREAK GROWS ON UPPER EAST SIDE

After testing 180 different cooling towers, the city found live or dead Legionella bacteria in 31 buildings. The city, in a statement, stressed that the discovery of dead bacteria in some buildings did not necessarily mean people in that building were ever exposed to the disease. 

The city ordered the following buildings, which includes the building that houses the vaunted Guggenheim Museum at 1071 Fifth Ave., to initiate a deep clean and disinfectant of their cooling towers "out of an abundance of caution.

City officials cautioned residents that mask wearing will likely not help stave off infection as Legionella bacteria is not transmissible person-to-person. Community cluster outbreaks like the Upper East Side one usually stem from common sources of exposure, such as "cooling towers, hot tubs, and spray fountains," the city said.

Public testing of the bacterium are ongoing, the city also announced. 

The city last experienced a major Legionnaires' outbreak in August 2025, when over 100 people in Harlem got sick and seven died. 

Fox News Digital contacted the NYC Health Department for additional comment.

item.title: Connor Bedard is all about the Blackhawks bringing back franchise great Patrick Kane, but is that a good idea?
item.description: Connor Bedard made a public pitch to lure Patrick Kane back to Chicago, while Chris Chelios claims Kane's decision is down to two teams.
item.content:encoded:
Patrick Kane — the top-scoring American-born player in NHL history — is still a free agent, and Chicago Blackhawks star Connor Bedard thinks it's time to bring Showtime back to Chi-Town.

The 37-year-old spent 15 seasons and change in Chicago after they drafted him first overall in the 2007 Draft, and went on to help the franchise win Stanley Cups in 2010, 2013, and 2015.

They traded him to the New York Rangers in 2023, which means he and Bedard have never played together, but with Kane on the open market, there's a real chance it could happen.

CONNOR BEDARD RE-SIGNS WITH BLACKHAWKS, ENDING HOPES FOR ANOTHER NHL OFFER SHEET THIS OFFSEASON

"I don't know what I can say to persuade his decision, but obviously we'd all be so excited to have a player like that, but also a guy like that," Bedard said, per NHL.com. "I can't imagine his first game back at the (United Center with the Blackhawks), the reaction he'd get and how much juice that would bring to not only our team but our fans, as well. That would be incredible to play with him and learn from him."

This comes as former Blackhawks defenseman Chris Chelios claimed on a Chicago sports radio show that Kane is down to either Chicago or Buffalo.

This is a good time to go to Buffalo, seeing as they just returned to the playoffs. Plus, Kane is from Buffalo, and he could do the same thing as his long-time Blackhawks running mate Jonathan Toews did with the Winnipeg Jets, and wrap up his career with his hometown team.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

However, they don't need him as the Blackhawks do.

Bedard is already going to miss the start of the season after undergoing shoulder surgery. If the Blackhawks want any prayer of keeping up with the murderers' row that is the Central Division, they need to start strong and be in the mix when Bedard comes back.

How better to do that than by adding a veteran presence who can still bring it offensively.

While Kane isn't producing like he was at his peak, he had 57 points (16G, 41A) in 67 games.

For a team that was second-to-last in goals for last season, it's probably worth bringing him in if he's up for it.

Chicago's projected cap space is $14.31 million at the moment, which would be more than plenty to bring in Kane, who made $3,000,000 last season on a one-year deal with the Red Wings.

Of course, the decision will be his, and if both the Sabres and Blackhawks want him, will he opt for the more likely playoff ticket, or bring things full circle and take on a bigger role within the team?
